title: Energy-Modulated Time-Asymmetric Spontaneous Collapse: Forward-Backward Dynamics from Stochastic Ito Reversal and Bright Solitons
authors: Ikechukwu C. Okoro, Mike O. Osiele, Godfrey E. Akpojotor
abstract: We present a rigorous theoretical framework for symmetry breaking and quantum irreversibility arising from stochastic Ito field reversal within a cubic-quintic nonlinear Schrodinger equation (CQ-NLSE) formalism. Starting from three physically motivated considerations, forward and backward nonlinear stochastic differential equations are derived via the Ito calculus. Kinematic time-reversal is shown to be fundamentally incompatible with the Ito stochastic structure, yielding the universal asymmetry-coupling parameter of 2/3. An energy-driven collapse operator proportional to the product of noise strength, local probability density, and excitation energy squared is introduced, amplifying the collapse in high-density, high-excitation regions. Exactly bright soliton solutions are obtained for a quasi-one-dimensional BEC of attractive Li-7 atoms, with forward and backward amplitude ratio of 1.870. Heat map analysis of the parameter planes reveals that the forward collapse operator grows monotonically in time while the backward counterpart decays, achieving a ratio approximately 1030, sharply distinguishing this framework from conventional symmetric collapse models.
summary: The paper proposes an energy-modulated, time-asymmetric spontaneous collapse model for BECs by analyzing stochastic Ito reversal on the CQ-NLSE. It demonstrates that forward and backward dynamics yield fundamentally different nonlinear terms ($\gamma_b = 5\gamma_f$), confirming a structural quantum irreversibility quantified by a universal asymmetry parameter of $2/3$.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental problem of quantum irreversibility by developing a time-asymmetric spontaneous collapse framework derived from stochastic Ito field reversal.
main_result: It establishes that the forward and backward dynamics exhibit strong asymmetry, quantified by $\gamma_b = 5\gamma_f$ and a universal asymmetry parameter of $2/3$.
method: The authors derive nonlinear stochastic differential equations (SDEs) using Ito calculus applied to the Cubic-Quintic Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (CQ-NLSE) formalism.
model_or_system: The model is applied to a quasi-one dimensional Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) of attractive $^7	ext{Li}$ atoms, utilizing the CQ-NLSE as its mean-field description.
key_observables: Forward and backward soliton amplitudes ratio ($A_f/A_b \approx 1.870$), collapse operator growth ($\Gamma_f$ grows while $\Gamma_b$ decays), and the asymmetry parameter $\gamma_{ap} = 2/3$.
important_parameters: Noise strength ($\eta$), local probability density ($|\Psi|^2$), excitation energy squared ($(\hbar\omega)^2$), and the universal asymmetry-coupling parameter (2/3).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the mean-field approximation, weak noise limits ($\eta \ll 1$), and an energy eigenvalue approximation for deriving the collapse operator $\Gamma$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spatial profiles of forward/backward solitons (amplitude ratio), plot the distinct quintic coupling parameters ($\gamma_b = 5\gamma_f$), and show heat maps confirming temporal growth asymmetry in the collapse operator.
paper_structure: The paper builds from deriving stochastic SDEs via Ito reversal, establishing the energy-driven collapse operator $\Gamma$, reducing to the CQ-NLSE mean-field limit, and finally analyzing the resulting time-asymmetric dynamics using soliton solutions and heat map diagnostics.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles measurement problem phenomenology within a quantum gas context (BEC), providing quantitative predictions for irreversible collapse mechanisms distinct from standard symmetric models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Forward (solid blue) and backward (solid orange) bright soliton profiles. Panel (a): real parts Re(Ψ); panel (b): imaginary parts Im(Ψ). Parameters: ω = 1.2, k = 1.8, t = 2.0.
Figure 2 presents the analytic collapse coupling strengths γf = 0.125ω (forward) and γb = 0.625ω (backward) as functions of ω, together with the collapse strength asymmetry parameter γap.

Figure 2. Forward collapse coupling γf = 0.125ω (solid blue), backward collapse coupling γb = 0.625ω (dashed red), and constant asymmetry parameter γap = 2/3 ≈0.667 (dash- dot black) as functions of ω. Note γb = 5γf at every frequency.

Figure 3 displays the complete (k, ω) parameter space landscape of both collapse operators simultaneously as a heat map with contour overlay, while Figure 4 shows their temporal evolution. The heat map reveals the full two dimensional structure of the collapse dynamics in a way that one dimensional slices cannot: the soliton boundary ω = k2/2 appears as a sharp transition from the hatched non-soliton re- gion (upper left) to the active collapse region (lower right), and the colour gradient immediately shows that Γf (panel a) attains higher values across the entire soliton-

Figure 3. Heat map and contour plot of the forward collapse operator Γf(k, ω) (panel a) and backward collapse operator Γb(k, ω) (panel b) in the (k, ω) parameter plane at t = 2.0. Colour encodes log10(Γ) on a shared scale; white contour lines label equal-value levels. The dashed white line is the soliton boundary ω = k2/2; the hatched region (k2 < 2ω) is the non-soliton domain where no bright soliton solutions exist. Both panels share the same colour scale, making the forward–backward asymmetry directly visible: Γf reaches higher values (brighter yellow) than Γb across the entire soliton-allowed region.
